topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The click of the mouse had been the last normal sound in my life. I’d stumbled upon it while deep-cleaning my old laptop, a forgotten relic from freshman year. There, nestled between outdated downloads and half-finished spreadsheets, was it: my personal essay, perfectly formatted, gleaming under the screen light, published on the university’s admissions page as a model submission. My hands began to shake, a frantic tremor starting in my fingertips and spreading up my arms. I leaned closer, my breath catching in my throat. The words, my carefully crafted narrative about navigating a difficult summer internship, were now a blueprint for someone else’s success. A cold, hard knot tightened in my chest, constricting my lungs. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the small room, my movements jerky and agitated. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring. I needed to find the administrator responsible, needed to demand an explanation for this brazen theft of my vulnerability.

[story 2]

It wasn't until I saw the publication date that the sickening realization hit me. My essay, the one I poured my soul into—the one detailing the quiet resilience I found while volunteering at the animal shelter—was not just displayed; it was *used*. It was sitting there, polished and presented as the pinnacle of applicant storytelling. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I felt a sudden, intense pressure behind my eyes, like a physical weight pressing down on my skull. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. I reread the accompanying boilerplate text, the official endorsement, and a wave of pure, corrosive anger washed over me. I snatched the printout, my knuckles white as I gripped the paper. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. This wasn't just an error; it felt like a deliberate, calculated slight against everything I had worked for. I needed to confront whoever had made this happen, to make them understand the sheer audacity of their action.

[story 3]

I found the link accidentally while scrolling through a generic university recruitment page. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat against the silence of the library. When the screen displayed my personal statement—my intimate reflection on overcoming self-doubt—and saw the little footnote indicating it was an 'exemplar piece' for prospective students, the air seemed to evacuate the room. I dropped the tablet onto the table with a clatter that sounded deafening. My entire body went rigid. I took a sharp, shallow breath, trying to regulate the sudden, violent surge of feeling that threatened to spill over. My vision narrowed; the edges of the room seemed to blur as my focus locked solely on that displayed text. A deep, simmering resentment began to churn in my stomach, acidic and relentless. I stood up abruptly, my chair scraping backward across the linoleum floor. I started walking toward the exit, my steps heavy and deliberate, a silent promise forming in my mind: this level of disrespect would not go unaddressed.
